one_line_takeaway: Averaging the weights of several reward models (rather than their predictions) yields a single, efficient RM that is more reliable under distribution shift and more robust to noisy preference labels, delaying reward hacking and producing policies that win ~79% vs a single-RM policy and ~99.8% vs SFT on TL;DR summarization.
key_finding: In RL fine-tuning on TL;DR (clean setup), a policy trained against WARM (M=6) achieves a 79.4% win rate under the oracle preference metric against a policy RL-fine-tuned with a single reward model (φ1, the best individual RM); the same WARM M=6 policy reaches a 99.8% win rate against the SFT initialization (the highest among all policies compared). Increasing M pushes the Pareto front up/left and delays reward-hacking collapse, though M=10 delays hacking without beating M=6's peak. In best-of-N, WARM attains up to a 92.5% win rate against random selection from SFT, and all competing selection strategies score below a 50% win rate against WARM M=6. On the diagnostic corruption experiment, WARM is far worse than ENS at fitting the 25%-corrupted training labels (i.e., it memorizes less — the desired behavior) yet generalizes better than ENS on OOD data.
core_contribution: The paper proposes WARM (Weight Averaged Reward Models), a method to mitigate reward hacking in RLHF. Instead of ensembling the predictions of multiple reward models (RMs), WARM fine-tunes M RMs from a shared pre-trained LLM (with different hyperparameters, data ordering, and a "Baklava" procedure that initializes from different SFT checkpoints for diversity) and then averages them in weight space by linear interpolation into a single RM. This is enabled by linear mode connectivity (LMC), which holds because of the shared pre-training. The paper's own claimed benefits versus prediction ensembling (ENS): (1) efficiency — one set of weights, no memory/inference overhead; (2) improved reliability under distribution shifts (variance reduction); and (3) improved robustness to label corruption, because weight averaging reduces memorization of corrupted/spurious labels whereas ENS memorizes them. Note: prediction ensembling (ENS) is prior work (Christiano et al.; cited [41,42]) used as the baseline, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: This is a safety/robustness paper, but the 'adversary' is not an external attacker. The threat is reward hacking (a.k.a. reward overoptimization): during RLHF, the RL policy being optimized exploits loopholes and misspecification in the proxy reward model to obtain high proxy rewards without satisfying true human preferences, degrading real quality (verbosity, sycophancy, bias) and, the paper argues, escalating into misalignment/safety risks. Two failure sources are targeted: (1) distribution shift, since policy generations drift OOD relative to the offline preference dataset as RL proceeds; and (2) inconsistent/noisy preference labels (low inter-labeler agreement, and an explicit 25% label-corruption stress test). WARM is a defense that hardens the reward signal against both, not a defense against a deliberate attacker.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) unlike prediction ensembling, WARM cannot combine RMs from different architectures/pre-trainings — it requires shared pre-training for LMC — and it forgoes ENS's prediction-disagreement uncertainty estimate; (2) it reduces memorization but does not eradicate spurious correlations/biases inherent in the preference data (e.g., if every individual RM relies on summary length, WARM replicates that bias); (3) WARM improves only reward modeling and does not address other RLHF challenges, so it must sit within broader responsible-AI efforts. Additional scope caveats from the text: experiments are confined to one task (TL;DR summarization) with small PaLM-XXS RMs and AI-generated (RLAIF) labels; on clean in-distribution training samples WA is slightly worse than ENS; and effectiveness depends on a delicate diversity-vs-LMC trade-off.

datasets_used: RLAIF-generated pairwise preference labels produced by a prompted PaLM-L (also used as the oracle preference metric)
A 25%-label-corruption variant of the preference dataset (corrupt setup)
quantitative_results: 79.4% oracle-preference win rate: policy RL-fine-tuned with WARM (M=6) vs policy RL-fine-tuned with a single RM (φ1), clean setup
99.8% win rate: WARM M=6 policy vs SFT initialization (highest among all policies)
Up to 92.5% best-of-N win rate for WARM vs random selection of a summary from SFT; all selection strategies score <50% vs WARM M=6
Corruption stress test uses 25% swapped binary labels; WARM memorizes corrupted train labels far less than ENS while generalizing better OOD
Control/oracle evaluation RM (PaLM-XS) reaches 80.1% accuracy on the OOD set D_ood — this is the measurement instrument, NOT a WARM performance result
72.6% inter-labeler agreement is a CITED figure for InstructGPT [40], not a result of this paper
baselines_compared: Single/individual reward models (e.g., φ1, the best individual RM on D_ood)
Prediction ensembling (ENS), averaging rewards from M RMs (M=2)
WARM with varying M (2, 6, 10) as an internal comparison
